findings.title: Rate-limiting requirement not implemented
findings.label: spec-gap
findings.claim: The spec requires rate-limiting of login attempts, but no rate-limit logic exists in the implementation.
findings.detail: The spec item 'Rate-limit attempts' is listed as a hard requirement to prevent brute-force attacks. The current implementation of the login handler in src/auth/login.ts makes no attempt to track failed attempts, enforce a lockout threshold, or return 429 responses. This leaves the endpoint fully open to credential-stuffing and brute-force attacks.
findings.suggestedFix: Add a sliding-window rate limiter (e.g. express-rate-limit or a Redis-backed counter) keyed on IP + username. Return HTTP 429 with Retry-After once the threshold is exceeded.

findings.title: Password compared with == allowing type coercion
findings.label: bug
findings.claim: Password compared with == allowing type coercion
findings.detail: Use a constant-time strict comparison; == permits type juggling. An attacker can craft an input that coerces to the expected password value, bypassing authentication entirely. In Node.js, '0' == false evaluates to true, so a numeric-coercible password input could slip through the equality check.
findings.suggestedFix: Use crypto.timingSafeEqual on Buffers derived from Buffer.from(input) and Buffer.from(stored) after hashing both with the same algorithm. This prevents both type-coercion bypasses and timing side-channels.

summary: Two findings identified in src/auth/login.ts: a missing rate-limiting requirement (spec-gap, medium) and a high-severity correctness bug where password comparison uses == instead of a constant-time strict equality, enabling type-coercion bypass. The high-severity finding drives the escalation to 'major'. Both findings were independently verified and unrefuted.
